story_summary: A personal account of lifelong commitment to community service through sustained financial giving, board leadership, volunteer work, and the ethic of "paying it forward."

context.role: Business executive and long-term community volunteer/board member
context.stakes: Community well-being, organizational effectiveness, personal legacy and relationships

core_conflict: Whether and how much to commit time, money, and leadership to community organizations—balancing personal/professional life with sustained, escalating public service.

decision_moments.trigger: Joining Peat Marwick and exposure to the firm's expectation of community involvement.
constraints: Cultural norm that many colleagues ignored the expectation
options: Ignore the firm's community expectation and focus on personal life
Make a modest, voluntary payroll-deduction donation
Seek deeper involvement in community organizations
decision_moments.choice: Made a modest payroll deduction to United Way from his first paycheck and committed to ongoing support.
decision_moments.reasoning: He was preconditioned by prior service and saw community involvement as interesting, a way to meet people, and worthwhile.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Ongoing financial commitment from early salary and allocation of attention to community activities
decision_moments.outcome: Established an uninterrupted pattern of United Way support beginning in 1963 that continued for decades.

decision_moments.trigger: Personal request from Bob Elmore in the 1980s to increase annual contribution.
constraints: Existing pledge level ($1,000 a year at that time)
Social expectation implied by the request
decision_moments.choice: Doubled the pledge in response to the request.
decision_moments.reasoning: Peer appeal and belief that he could and should do more when asked.
decision_moments.outcome: Higher annual giving and continued escalation of contributions over time.

decision_moments.trigger: Client Pete Musser encouraging higher giving while in Philadelphia.
options: Increase contribution to a substantial new level
decision_moments.reasoning: Responded to external promptings and a desire to do more as financial position improved.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Commitment to a significantly larger annual donation
decision_moments.outcome: At least $10,000 contributed every year since that increase and membership in Tocqueville Society.

decision_moments.trigger: Requests and invitations to serve on nonprofit boards and leadership roles.
constraints: Responsibilities of professional and family life
decision_moments.choice: Accepted board memberships and multiple chair positions (Museum of Science and Discovery, Broward Library Foundation, Community Foundation of Broward, etc.).
decision_moments.reasoning: Believed business experience could help organizations and saw these roles as learning laboratories for leadership.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Significant time commitment and accountability for organizational outcomes
decision_moments.outcome: Developed leadership skills, influenced local institutions, and formed lasting relationships.

decision_moments.trigger: 2013 Oklahoma City tornado and resulting need for cleanup assistance.
constraints: Physical demand and limited personal time
options: Travel to assist directly with cleanup
decision_moments.choice: Traveled to Oklahoma City and spent a week helping with the cleanup.
decision_moments.reasoning: Desire to do tangible, hands-on good for people in need.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Physical exhaustion and time away from home/professional duties
decision_moments.outcome: Came home physically weary but with a strong sense of having done good: "I came home physically weary but with a wonderful inner feeling."

principles.principle: Sustained, consistent giving builds long-term community impact.
Volunteer leadership roles are opportunities to apply professional skills and to learn leadership.
principles.evidence: I was able to bring some of my business training and experience to bear on the organizations—but I learned a lot from them in return.

principles.principle: Combine monetary support with time and leadership for greater impact.
principles.evidence: Our involvement, however, has been more than just monetary contributions. We have contributed our time and energy as well.

principles.principle: Adopt an ethic of reciprocity—'pay it forward'—as a guiding motive for service.
principles.evidence: Dot and I have adopted that as our essential creed throughout the years.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When company culture expects community involvement, participate to build networks and experience.
decision_heuristics.evidence: management of the firm expected everyone to be involved in the community in some way.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When a trusted peer asks you to increase your support, consider promptly raising your pledge.
Use board service to transfer business expertise to nonprofits and treat it as a learning laboratory.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I was able to bring some of my business training and experience to bear on the organizations—but I learned a lot from them in return.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When disaster relief needs hands, volunteer on-site even if physically demanding.
decision_heuristics.evidence: spent a week in the blazing sun helping with the cleanup.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Gradually escalate financial support as personal financial position improves.
decision_heuristics.evidence: We gradually escalated our United Way contributions over time, trying to do a little better every year.

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Willing to accept moderate financial and personal-time costs to achieve community impact.
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Collaborative and responsive to peer requests; relationship-building through service.
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Proactive—accepts formal leadership roles (chairman, board member) and ongoing committee work.
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Reciprocity/pay-it-forward ethic; service as moral responsibility for the fortunate.

quotes.quote: We make a living by what we do, but we make a life by what we give.
community service has actually become a way of life for me.
Dot and I have adopted that as our essential creed throughout the years.
